answer:
**In Old Javanese (Kawi), the Sanskrit term *Śiva*—the proper name of the Hindu deity—is borrowed directly and fully naturalized into the religious lexicon without phonological alteration or semantic shift.**[^1][^2]

It appears consistently as *śiva* (in Latin transliteration of the Kawi script), prefixed with native honorifics like *bhaṭāra* ("lord") to form *bhaṭāra śiva* ("Lord Śiva"), and integrated into compounds such as *śivaliṅga* ("Śiva-liṅga"), *śivadvāra* ("gate of Śiva"), and *sadāśiva* ("eternal Śiva").[^3][^4] This usage mirrors its Sanskrit role as the name of the supreme deity in Śaiva tantric contexts, denoting the same divine entity without change in referent (e.g., the ultimate reality, teacher of gods, or abode of liberation).[^5][^6]

Kawi, as a literary register heavily influenced by Sanskrit (especially in ritual, philosophical, and tantric texts like those in the corpus), routinely incorporates such loanwords verbatim for sacred terms, treating them as prestige vocabulary rather than "foreign."[^7] There is no evidence here of hyperforeignism (e.g., archaic pronunciation markers), nativization (e.g., to plain *siva* without ś), or semantic extension (e.g., to non-deity meanings). The corpus lacks metalinguistic commentary on loanword adaptation, but usage confirms seamless integration as a core theonym.[^8]

[^1]: *Gaṇapatitattva*, ch. 1 (transliteration), [para. p-780ce0](/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/transliteration/#p-780ce0) (*bhaṭāra śiva*); (translation), [para. p-bbfaeb](/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/translation/#p-bbfaeb) ("Śiva").
[^2]: *San Hyaṅ Mahājñāna*, ch. 1 (transliteration), [para. p-3900af](/works/unknown-san-hyan-mahajnana-11c531/01/transliteration/#p-3900af) (*śiwāya*); (translation), [para. p-c397bf](/works/unknown-san-hyan-mahajnana-11c531/01/translation/#p-c397bf) ("Lord Shiva").
[^3]: *Gaṇapatitattva*, ch. 1 (transliteration), [para. p-4ae280](/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/transliteration/#p-4ae280) (*śivadvāra*); [para. p-735f92](/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/transliteration/#p-735f92) (*śivaliṅga*).
[^4]: *Gaṇapatitattva*, ch. 1 (translation), [ch. URL](/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/translation/).
[^5]: *San Hyaṅ Mahājñāna*, ch. 1 (translation), [para. p-94a500](/works/unknown-san-hyan-mahajnana-11c531/01/translation/#p-94a500) (Śiva as "master of the entire world").
[^6]: *Gaṇapatitattva*, ch. 1 (translation), [para. p-001434](/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/translation/#p-001434) (Śiva as "giver of success," "teacher to the host of gods").
[^7]: Both works are Śaiva tantras in Kawi; cf. corpus metadata for "unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136" and "unknown-san-hyan-mahajnana-11c531" (*language: Kawi*, *genre: Indic*, *era: Medieval*).
[^8]: No paragraphs discuss loanword treatment; search yields only theophoric uses (*siva/śiva/shiva*: 5 hits, all deictic). The 37-work corpus covers limited Kawi (9 works); broader linguistics unavailable here.
